articles.title: Red alert in Mumbai: Heavy rain batters city on monsoon's first morning; Andheri underpass shut - Moneycontrol.com

articles.full_text:
The alert came after a night of heavy downpour that began soon after the southwest monsoon finally made its way into Mumbai on Tuesday, a full 13 days behind schedule.
- IMD issues red alert for Mumbai, Palghar after heavy rain
- Western suburbs record highest rainfall at 113 mm in 24 hours
- Suburban trains and BEST buses run normally despite waterlogging
Did our AI summary help?
Mumbai woke up to relentless rain on Wednesday, June 24, as the India Meteorological Department (IMD) issued a red alert for the city and neighbouring Palghar district, warning of intense to very intense spells of rainfall accompanied by thunderstorms, lightning and gusty winds touching 40 to 60 kmph.
The alert came after a night of heavy downpour that began soon after the southwest monsoon finally made its way into Mumbai on Tuesday, a full 13 days behind schedule. The city's normal onset date for the monsoon is June 10, and this year's delay, while notable, was still short of the historic late arrivals recorded in June 1958 and 1974, when the monsoon held off until June 28.
The Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation, in a post on X, said isolated places across Mumbai and Palghar were likely to witness thunderstorms with lightning and intense to very intense spells of rain, along with gusty winds reaching 40 to 60 kmph.
The weather warning extended beyond Mumbai. Thane district has been placed under an orange alert, while Raigad, Ratnagiri and Sindhudurg districts are under a yellow alert, as per News18. Kalyan, Dombivli, Karjat and Neral had been recording heavy to very heavy rainfall since late Tuesday night, it added.
Rainfall data for the 24 hours between 8 am on June 23 and 2 am on June 24 showed the city received an average of 78 mm of rain. The eastern suburbs recorded 87 mm, while the western suburbs bore the brunt with 113 mm, the highest across the metropolitan region.
The intensity peaked in the early hours of Wednesday. Between 1 am and 2 am alone, several locations recorded sharp spikes in rainfall. In the city area, Kala Killa in Dharavi logged 19 mm, Adarsh Nagar School in Worli recorded 16 mm, and the Dadar and Worli fire stations each saw 15 mm during the hour, Midday reported.
The eastern suburbs fared worse during the same window, with the Bhandup Complex recording the highest rainfall at 40 mm, followed by the S Ward at 35 mm, T Ward at 31 mm and Mithagar Municipal School at 29 mm, the report said. In the western suburbs, Anand Nagar Municipal School recorded 32 mm, while Dindoshi Vasahat and Dahisar each logged 30 mm, and the Malawani fire station recorded 26 mm, as per Midday report.
Civic authorities said the Mithi river, often a flashpoint during heavy rain, was flowing at 1.83 metres, comfortably below the danger mark despite the sustained downpour, the report added.
An underpass in Andheri was shut after it flooded, with civic officials stationed at the site to keep vehicles from entering, the report said. The precaution proved necessary, as a rickshaw driver who attempted to cross got stranded in the middle of the submerged subway, prompting two civic workers to wade in and pull him to safety. A BMC official said his life had been in danger, News18 reported.
Despite the disruption caused by the rain, Mumbai's suburban railway network and BEST bus services continued to function normally through the morning rush hours, offering some relief to commuters, as per Midday.
The IMD has urged residents to exercise caution, steer clear of waterlogged areas and stay updated through advisories issued by local authorities, with heavy rainfall activity expected to persist over the coming hours, the report concluded.

articles.title: Mumbai Weather Latest Update: Heavy Rain Triggers Waterlogging and Traffic Delays; Trees Uprooted, No Casualties Reported - NewsX
articles.full_text:
The Southwest Monsoon officially hit Mumbai on June 23, thirteen days after its normal onset date of June 10. While the arrival of the rains brought much-needed relief from the sweltering heat, the continuous overnight downpour has triggered widespread waterlogging and traffic delays across several parts of the city.
Mumbai Weather Latest News: Heavy Rains Trigger Waterlogging, Traffic Snarls
Visuals from across Mumbai show flooded roads, submerged railway tracks, and closed subways, with manholes jammed due to the excessive rush of water from the streets. While trees have been uprooted in many areas and several vehicles have been damaged by the heavy rainfall, fortunately, no major injuries or structural casualties have been reported so far. Residents are strongly advised to remain calm, stay indoors, and check official updates from the Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC) before planning any travel.
Mumbai Weather: Trans-Harbour Line Disrupted After Track Cave-In
Central Railway operations faced major disruptions after an excessive flow of rainwater caused a track cave-in between the Koparkhairane and Turbhe stations on the Trans-Harbour line. Railway officials declared the Up line (towards Thane) unsafe at 5:06 AM immediately after the cave-in was spotted. Meanwhile, restoration work cleared the Down line by 7:35 AM, making it safe for operations. Trains on this section are currently permitted to run at a restricted speed of 30 kmph.
Mumbai Weather Latest Update: IMD Issues Red Alert for Mumbai and Palghar
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a Red Alert for Mumbai, its suburbs, and Palghar on June 24, warning of extremely heavy rainfall. Concurrently, an Orange Alert has been issued for Thane, while a Yellow Alert remains in place for Raigad, Ratnagiri, and Sindhudurg. Furthermore, the weather department has issued an immediate short-term warning for nearby regions, forecasting thunderstorms accompanied by gusty winds reaching speeds of 40–60 kmph.
